addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by dropping filler, articles, and pleasantries while keeping full technical accuracy. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman.
- diagnose: Disciplined diagnosis loop for hard bugs and performance regressions. Reproduce → minimise → hypothesise → instrument → fix → regression-test. Use when user says "diagnose this" / "debug this", reports a bug, says something is broken/throwing/failing, or describes a performance regression.
- grill-me: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design until reaching shared understanding, resolving each branch of the decision tree. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan, get grilled on their design, or mentions "grill me".
- grill-with-docs: Grilling session that challenges your plan against the existing domain model, sharpens terminology, and updates documentation (CONTEXT.md, ADRs) inline as decisions crystallise. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan against their project's language and documented decisions.
- handoff: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.
- improve-codebase-architecture: Find deepening opportunities in a codebase, informed by the domain language in CONTEXT.md and the decisions in docs/adr/. Use when the user wants to improve architecture, find refactoring opportunities, consolidate tightly-coupled modules, or make a codebase more testable and AI-navigable.
- prototype: Build a throwaway prototype to flesh out a design before committing to it. Routes between two branches — a runnable terminal app for state/business-logic questions, or several radically different UI variations toggleable from one route. Use when the user wants to prototype, sanity-check a data model or state machine, mock up a UI, explore design options, or says "prototype this", "let me play with it", "try a few designs".
- tdd: Test-driven development with red-green-refactor loop. Use when user wants to build features or fix bugs using TDD, mentions "red-green-refactor", wants integration tests, or asks for test-first development.
- to-prd: Turn the current conversation context into a PRD and publish it to the project issue tracker. Use when user wants to create a PRD from the current context.
- triage: Triage issues through a state machine driven by triage roles. Use when user wants to create an issue, triage issues, review incoming bugs or feature requests, prepare issues for an AFK agent, or manage issue workflow.
- volume-sync: Sync data between the local /dev3 volume (ap-southeast-4) and the remote 5TB volume (us-east-2). Use when user says "sync volume", "pull from us-east-2", "push to us-east-2", or invokes /volume-sync.
- write-a-skill: Create new agent skills with proper structure, progressive disclosure, and bundled resources. Use when user wants to create, write, or build a new skill.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# AWS Trn2 Neuron DLAMI runbook
2	
3	This repo treats AWS Trn2 as a local OpenAI-compatible inference service. The
4	Python project environment stays CPU/client-side; Neuron vLLM is launched from
5	the Neuron DLAMI environment.
6	
7	**NEW**: We now support **Docker-based deployment** using the official AWS Neuron Deep Learning Container. See [Neuron Docker Quick Start](neuron_docker_quickstart.md) for the simplified Docker workflow, or continue reading for the native DLAMI setup.
8	
9	Official references:
10	
11	- AWS Neuron vLLM user guide: https://awsdocs-neuron.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/libraries/nxd-inference/developer_guides/vllm-user-guide.html
12	- AWS Neuron PyTorch setup: https://awsdocs-neuron.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/general/setup/torch-neuronx.html
13	- AWS Trn2 architecture: https://awsdocs-neuron.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/about-neuron/arch/neuron-hardware/trn2-arch.html
14	- AWS Neuron DLC: https://github.com/aws-neuron/deep-learning-containers
15	
16	## Environment
17	
18	Use the default repo environment for clients, retrieval, and evaluation control:
19	
20	```bash
21	uv sync
22	source .venv/bin/activate
23	```
24	
25	Do not install `flash-attn`, CUDA vLLM, or CUDA Docker images in this default
26	environment. The old CUDA setup remains available with:
27	
28	```bash
29	uv sync --extra cuda
30	```
31	
32	On the DLAMI, verify Neuron visibility before serving models:
33	
34	```bash
35	neuron-ls
36	neuron-top
37	```
38	
39	## Serve required models
40	
41	Each script preserves the existing model id. If Neuron vLLM cannot compile or
42	serve that model, treat it as an unsupported-model blocker; do not silently swap
43	models or route to an external API.
44	
45	```bash
46	bash bash/neuron/serve_tongyi.bash
47	bash bash/neuron/serve_gpt_oss_20b.bash
48	bash bash/neuron/serve_gpt_oss_120b.bash
49	bash bash/neuron/serve_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
50	```
51	
52	Common overrides:
53	
54	```bash
55	TP_SIZE=64 MAX_MODEL_LEN=32768 MAX_NUM_SEQS=1 PORT=8032 \
56	  bash bash/neuron/serve_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
57	```
58	
59	The generic launcher is `bash/neuron/serve_vllm_neuron.bash`. It sets
60	`VLLM_NEURON_FRAMEWORK=neuronx-distributed-inference` and calls
61	`python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server`. The Neuron vLLM platform
62	plugin selects the Neuron backend.
63	
64	The wrapper scripts and generic launcher accept these environment variables:
65	
66	- `MODEL`: Hugging Face model id or an explicit local checkpoint path.
67	- `SERVED_MODEL_NAME`: model name exposed by the OpenAI-compatible API. It
68	  defaults to the original `MODEL`, even when weights are loaded from the
69	  repo-local cache.
70	- `TOKENIZER`: tokenizer id or path. Auto-cached model weights default to the
71	  original model id because NxD's model serialization does not save tokenizer
72	  files.
73	- `HOST` and `PORT`: listen address and TCP port.
74	- `TP_SIZE`: number of NeuronCores used for tensor parallelism. It is part of
75	  the compiled-artifact cache key.
76	- `MAX_MODEL_LEN`: compiled context capacity; larger values make compilation
77	  and KV-cache requirements grow.
78	- `MAX_NUM_SEQS`: maximum concurrent sequences. This is compile-time capacity,
79	  not the client's thread count.
80	- `BLOCK_SIZE`: vLLM KV-cache block size.
81	- `NUM_GPU_BLOCKS_OVERRIDE`: effective vLLM KV-block capacity before the
82	  Neuron plugin adds its reserved null block.
83	- `ENABLE_PREFIX_CACHING`: pass the explicit vLLM prefix-caching switch.
84	- `ENABLE_CHUNKED_PREFILL`: pass the explicit vLLM chunked-prefill switch.
85	- `NEURON_SCHEDULER`: Tongyi-only scheduler profile. `custom` (the default)
86	  uses Neuron continuous batching and disables chunked prefill. `native` sets
87	  `DISABLE_NEURON_CUSTOM_SCHEDULER=1`, enables chunked prefill, and defaults
88	  `MAX_NUM_BATCHED_TOKENS` to `16384` to match the compiled uncached-context
89	  bucket. Compile and validate `native` with a separate artifact/cache before
90	  using it in a run; an explicit `--max-num-batched-tokens` in
91	  `EXTRA_VLLM_ARGS` takes precedence.
92	- `OVERRIDE_NEURON_CONFIG`: JSON object passed through vLLM's
93	  `additional_config.override_neuron_config` interface to NxD.
94	- `EXTRA_VLLM_ARGS`: additional vLLM CLI arguments. Keep quoting simple because
95	  the launcher splits this string into shell words.
96	- `NEURON_CACHE_TAG`: manual compiled-cache invalidation tag.
97	- `MAX_NUM_BATCHED_TOKENS`: native-scheduler chunked-prefill limit. Leave it
98	  at the default `16384` for the TP16 128k profile unless testing a separately
99	  compiled bucket configuration.
100	- `NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS`: advanced explicit compiled-artifact directory
101	  override.
102	- `NXD_COMPILE_MAX_WORKERS`: maximum concurrent NxD HLO compiler jobs. The
103	  Tongyi precompile wrapper defaults to `1`; direct serving leaves NxD unchanged
104	  unless this variable is set. The launcher installs the limit inside vLLM's
105	  spawned EngineCore process, where NxD creates the compiler executor. The
106	  default compile profile serializes HLO compilation to reduce memory pressure.
107	- `NEURON_COMPILE_MEMORY_HIGH` and `NEURON_COMPILE_MEMORY_MAX`: soft and hard
108	  cgroup limits used by `compile_tongyi.bash`. They default to `1750G` and
109	  `1900G` on the 2 TiB Trn2.48xlarge host.
110	- `NEURON_COMPILE_USE_SYSTEMD_SCOPE`: set to `0` only when the compile already
111	  runs under an equivalent cgroup limit. It defaults to `1`.
112	- `PYTHON_BIN`: Python executable from the Neuron environment.
113	
114	The server exposes the OpenAI-compatible HTTP interface. Readiness/model
115	identity is available at `GET /v1/models`; Tongyi and the judge use
116	`POST /v1/chat/completions`. `n_tongyi.bash` starts each server, waits for its
117	`/v1/models` endpoint, then runs the client or evaluator against that base URL.
118	
119	## Model and compilation caches
120	
121	The generic launcher keeps both cache layers under the ignored
122	`local-models/` directory:
123	
124	- Hugging Face weights are stored at `local-models/<model-id>/`. Once the config
125	  and either a single weight file or a weight index with every referenced shard
126	  exist, later launches use that checkpoint instead of loading and saving the
127	  remote model again.
128	  Tongyi's tokenizer remains on the original model id because its locally
129	  serialized model directory contains weights but not tokenizer files. The
130	  Qwen3-32B download includes tokenizer files, and judge replicas use that local
131	  tokenizer path.
132	- Traced and compiled Neuron models are stored under
133	  `local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/<configuration-hash>/`. The hash
134	  includes the model id, tensor parallel size, sequence length, concurrency,
135	  block size, Neuron overrides, extra vLLM arguments, cache tag, and relevant
136	  package versions.
137	
138	The first launch still performs weight conversion and Neuron compilation.
139	Later launches with exactly the same model and compilation settings reuse the
140	compiled artifacts. Changing a compilation setting creates a separate cache
141	directory instead of reusing an incompatible model.
142	
143	Precompile Tongyi:
144	
145	```bash
146	bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi.bash
147	```
148	
149	The wrapper serializes Tongyi precompiles with `flock` and runs the complete
150	compiler process tree in a user systemd scope. The hard memory limit terminates
151	the compile rather than allowing concurrent `neuronx-cc` processes to make the
152	instance unresponsive. It writes timestamped `compile.log`, `kernel.log`, and
153	PID files under `logs/neuron-debug/`. Compile work and the persistent compiler
154	cache live under `local-models/` on `/dev3`, avoiding the root filesystem.
155	The priority HLO and graph tracing remain serial; the default profile keeps HLO
156	compilation serial to reduce peak memory.
157	
158	Before compiling, the wrapper moves inactive failed compiler-cache modules to a
159	timestamped sibling `.failed-*` directory. It does not touch complete modules
160	or a module whose lock is held by an active compiler. An incomplete repo-local
161	compiled-artifact directory is similarly renamed with a `.failed-<timestamp>`
162	suffix before the clean retry.
163	
164	The startup log reports a compiled cache as `cold` when the directory is empty,
165	`incomplete` when an interrupted compile left partial files, and `ready` only
166	when NxD's complete `model.pt` artifact exists. NxD still performs its own load
167	validation before serving requests.
168	
169	Set `NEURON_CACHE_TAG` when a model revision or local compiler-related change
170	is not represented by the normal launch arguments:
171	
172	```bash
173	NEURON_CACHE_TAG=tongyi-revision-2 bash bash/neuron/serve_tongyi.bash
174	```
175	
176	`NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS` remains an advanced explicit override. Use it only
177	for one model and one fixed configuration; vLLM Neuron loads a valid artifact
178	from that path even when the current launch arguments differ. Do not point
179	Tongyi and the Qwen judge at the same explicit directory.
180	
181	## Smoke test a server
182	
183	```bash
184	curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:8032/v1/models
185	
186	python - <<'PY'
187	from openai import OpenAI
188	client = OpenAI(base_url="http://127.0.0.1:8032/v1", api_key="EMPTY")
189	print(client.chat.completions.create(
190	    model="Qwen/Qwen3-32B",
191	    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Return exactly: ok"}],
192	    max_tokens=8,
193	).choices[0].message.content)
194	PY
195	```
196	
197	## Run Tongyi agent on Trn2
198	
199	Compile the three models sequentially. Do not run two compile scripts at the
200	same time: each compiler can use most of the host's memory.
201	
202	Compile Tongyi once:
203	
204	```bash
205	bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi.bash
206	```
207	
208	Use compile-only mode when NeuronCores are already serving an experiment:
209	
210	```bash
211	bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi_tp16.bash --compile-only
212	```
213	
214	This still starts vLLM's EngineCore as the NxDI configuration and compilation
215	entry point, but exits after `model.pt` and any configured pre-sharded weights
216	are written. It does not load the artifact onto NeuronCores, warm up the model,
217	or start the temporary HTTP server. The default mode retains end-to-end load
218	validation. Tongyi enables `save_sharded_checkpoint` by default, so a successful
219	compile also writes `weights/tp*_sharded_checkpoint.safetensors`; subsequent
220	servers load those TP shards instead of re-reading and sharding the full
221	Hugging Face checkpoint.
222	
223	The compiler writes the validated artifact directory to
224	`logs/neuron-debug/<compile-tag>-<timestamp>/compiled_artifact_path` and prints
225	it at completion. Use that exact path for a native runtime; do not infer it
226	from a cache directory or reuse the custom artifact.
227	
228	The default Tongyi artifact uses TP=8, `MAX_MODEL_LEN=128000`, and
229	`MAX_NUM_SEQS=2`, with context/suffix bucket `[16384]`, prefix
230	`[114688]`, and generation
231	`[16384, 32768, 65536, 98304, 128000]`.
232	
233	After Tongyi finishes, set up and compile the dedicated Qwen3 embedding
234	environment:
235	
236	```bash
237	bash bash/neuron/setup_qwen3_embedding_env.bash
238	bash bash/neuron/download_qwen3_embedding.bash
239	bash bash/neuron/compile_qwen3_embedding.bash
240	```
241	
242	After the embedding compile finishes, download and compile the judge:
243	
244	```bash
245	bash bash/neuron/download_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
246	bash bash/neuron/compile_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
247	```
248	
249	Do not start the experiment until all three compile scripts have completed.
250	Run a one-query smoke profile first:
251	
252	```bash
253	MAX_QUERIES=1 \
254	TONGYI_REPLICAS=1 \
255	JUDGE_REPLICAS=1 \
256	NUM_THREADS=1 \
257	JUDGE_NUM_THREADS=1 \
258	RUN_NAME=neuron_smoke \
259	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
260	```
261	
262	Then run the formal throughput profile:
263	
264	```bash
265	bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
266	```
267	
268	This profile enables `--filter-all` and registers only the `search` tool; it
269	does not enable `get_document`. The default run name is
270	`tongyi_filter_all_no_get`.
271	
272	The current full profile defaults to 18 client threads and starts three TP16
273	Tongyi replicas on ports `6008-6010`, using NeuronCores `0-47`; the TP8
274	Qwen3-Embedding-8B service uses cores `56-63`. Replica processes are
275	NUMA-bound and at most two replicas load concurrently. Each endpoint has two
276	LLM-call slots. With the current `custom` scheduler, the full profile defaults
277	to `PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=trajectory`: its 16k uncached-context bucket cannot
278	safely serve a long cold history after an APC miss. This is not merely an
279	endpoint-routing constraint. Completed requests release their KV blocks into
280	the endpoint's evictable prefix cache; with 8000 32-token blocks, a TP16
281	replica has room for only two 128k histories. Releasing six long custom
282	trajectories per endpoint would let their APC entries evict each other. The
283	separately compiled `native`/chunked profile defaults to
284	`PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=request`, which reserves a slot only for an LLM HTTP call
285	and releases it while the agent retrieves documents. That mode prefers the
286	trajectory's home endpoint for 0.2 seconds by default
287	(`PLANNING_AFFINITY_TIMEOUT`) before routing to another available replica.
288	
289	The lease scopes have intentionally different failure behavior. In
290	`custom`/`trajectory` mode, an `EngineDeadError` (or a failed post-connection
291	`/v1/models` probe) retires the dead endpoint and marks that query as
292	`[Failed]`; it never retries that long trajectory on a different replica,
293	which would make another cold prefill and can cascade the same crash. In
294	`native`/`request` mode, the failed call is retried on a surviving endpoint.
295	New trajectories skip retired endpoints in both modes, and all-retired pools
296	fail fast rather than consuming the 150-minute per-query timeout.
297	Failed output JSONs retain the error and `status: "failed"`, so a later clean
298	invocation reruns them instead of treating them as completed cached qids. The
299	resume scan also retries legacy empty `No answer found after 2h30mins` outputs,
300	which is how the old client represented a dead endpoint after exhausting HTTP
301	retries.
302	
303	The 2026-07-15 custom-scheduler run provides a useful but incomplete baseline:
304	before the replicas failed, its 10-second vLLM samples had 84.68% aggregate
305	slot duty (4510 occupied slots / 5326 possible slots). Per-replica duty was
306	79.35% (6008), 83.18% (6009), and 87.14% (6010). This shows that 18 client
307	threads were already enough to keep most of the six admitted LLM slots busy;
308	more threads alone cannot remove the remaining bubbles and cannot repair a
309	dead endpoint. It is historical slot-duty evidence only, not MFU or a valid
310	end-to-end throughput result: all three replicas later exited after a long
311	cold prefill exceeded the 16k suffix bucket.
312	
313	The detailed vLLM samples make the remaining limitation concrete: of the
314	53,202 sampled slot-seconds, **8,130 slot-seconds (15.28%)** had an unfilled
315	slot while `Waiting: 0 reqs`. This is a client/admission-bubble proxy: vLLM had
316	no request queued to use the empty slot, consistent with trajectories retaining
317	their endpoint capacity while fetching documents. It is not Tensor Engine idle
318	time. The remaining 29,718 slot-seconds were unobserved/dead capacity after
319	replica failure. A custom affinity lease is unsafe for this profile: returning
320	to the same endpoint does not pin its completed KV blocks, so more than two
321	long histories can evict each other and recreate the cold-prefill failure.
322	`n_tongyi.bash` rejects it before server start. The substantive safe step for
323	removing the live bubble is a verified native/request scheduler A/B.
324	
325	When the same logs are measured over one common window—from first planning
326	traffic at 03:40:30 to the last surviving replica's crash at 07:30:50—the
327	failure-inclusive planning slot-equivalent duty is only **54.34%**. This is the
328	right figure for lost planning core-time; the 84.68% figure only answers how
329	busy replicas were while each one was still alive. The corresponding **40.76%**
330	instance figure excludes the embedding service because it has no vLLM
331	`Running` log. Neither is MFU.
332	
333	### Fill the final eight planning cores (custom trajectory mode)
334	
335	The 3xTP16 planning topology leaves NeuronCores `48-55` unassigned. A verified
336	TP8, `MAX_NUM_SEQS=1`, 128k artifact exists at
337	`local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/9a36595723d1059555571d3bf524fa88a507814a2efbe2917865b6350bb75912`.
338	Set `ENABLE_TP8_SIDECAR=1` to start it on port `6011` and cores `48-55` after
339	the three TP16 replicas and embedding service are ready. Its one slot is
340	advertised separately, so the client schedules `6008:2,6009:2,6010:2,6011:1`
341	rather than over-admitting the sidecar. This raises planning capacity from six
342	to seven admitted sequences and assigns all 64 cores (56 planning + 8
343	embedding).
344	
345	```bash
346	ENABLE_TP8_SIDECAR=1 RUN_NAME=tongyi_tp16x3_tp8x1 \
347	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
348	```
349	
350	This is deliberately restricted to `NEURON_SCHEDULER=custom` with
351	`PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=trajectory`: a request-level lease could send a long
352	history to the TP8 replica without its APC state and reproduce the cold-prefill
353	crash. Do not enable it for the native/request A/B until a separate native TP8
354	artifact has passed smoke testing.
355	
356	The original TP4, 16k-suffix profile is not viable: its 2026-07-15 compile
357	failed with `NCC_EOOM002`, reporting 31.17 GB peak HBM against the 24.00 GB
358	Trn2 partition limit. A narrower TP4×1 profile with an 8k suffix and 120k
359	prefix did later compile to
360	`local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/27b98ea6f3cabed3fd9ed630d1a2996e28998618d11e43538edbd3d3e09c3586`.
361	An even narrower 4k suffix / 124k prefix artifact also compiled to
362	`local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/1564e9cb322946a1b12565ab27e5c4b63f2c8d1ee73a0f8b0111c01a6fdc1fd2`,
363	but the 8k profile is the better first experiment because a search tool result
364	can exceed a 4k uncached suffix. Both compiles used `--compile-only`, so neither
365	is a validated runtime topology. Smoke the 8k artifact on one copy before
366	considering two TP4 sidecars on cores `48-51` and `52-55`:
367	
368	```bash
369	PORT=6011 NEURON_RT_VISIBLE_CORES=48-51 \
370	NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS=local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/27b98ea6f3cabed3fd9ed630d1a2996e28998618d11e43538edbd3d3e09c3586 \
371	NEURON_REQUIRE_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS=1 \
372	  bash bash/neuron/serve_tongyi_tp4_s1.bash
373	```
374	
375	Only after a warm request succeeds without an HBM allocation failure or a
376	`Prefill len ... exceeds largest bucket` error should TP4×2 be compared with
377	the proven TP8×1 sidecar. `ENABLE_TP4_SIDECARS=1` starts one TP4 sidecar on each
378	four-core range, advertises their one-slot capacities separately, and is
379	mutually exclusive with the TP8 helper:
380	
381	```bash
382	ENABLE_TP4_SIDECARS=1 NUM_THREADS=18 RUN_EVAL=0 \
383	RUN_NAME=tongyi_tp16x3_tp4x2 \
384	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
385	```
386	
387	This turns the same eight otherwise unused planning cores into two slots, for
388	eight total planning slots (versus seven for TP8×1 and six without a sidecar).
389	It is an experimental throughput A/B, not a claimed speedup: TP4 may have
390	lower per-slot speed, so compare successful q/min, no-queue vacancy, and
391	slot-equivalent core-time before adopting it.
392	
393	For an externally managed A/B, start all planning servers first, then use
394	`START_SERVER=0`; the runner waits for every port listed in `PLANNING_PORTS`
395	before launching clients:
396	
397	```bash
398	START_SERVER=0 PLANNING_PORTS=6008-6012 \
399	PLANNING_ENDPOINT_CAPACITIES=6008:2,6009:2,6010:2,6011:1,6012:1 \
400	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
401	```
402	
403	FAISS remains CPU-side, while query embeddings come from the local Neuron HTTP
404	service. Override `START_SERVER=0` only when all planning and embedding
405	endpoints are already running. After generation completes, `n_tongyi.bash`
406	stops the Tongyi and embedding cluster, starts 16 independent TP4 judge
407	replicas on ports `8032-8047` using all 64 NeuronCores, and evaluates with 16
408	concurrent judge requests. Generation and judge replicas never occupy the
409	NeuronCores at the same time.
410	
411	### Measure planning core-time and MFU
412	
413	`Running: N reqs` in a vLLM server log measures admitted request slots, not
414	Neuron-core utilization. Report it as **slot duty** only:
415	
416	```text
417	slot duty = sum(Running) / (MAX_NUM_SEQS * number of samples)
418	```
419	
420	Use the checked-in summarizer for an experiment-window calculation that counts
421	time after a crashed replica as zero duty:
422	
423	```bash
424	python scripts/neuron_summarize_vllm_logs.py --year 2026 \
425	  logs/neuron-servers/<run>/tongyi-6008.log \
426	  logs/neuron-servers/<run>/tongyi-6009.log \
427	  logs/neuron-servers/<run>/tongyi-6010.log
428	```
429	
430	It reports **slot-equivalent core-time**, not MFU. It infers at most 30 seconds
431	from a scheduler sample and uses the first and final samples as the default
432	window; pass explicit `--window-start` and `--window-end` if the experiment
433	window is wider. When a log includes vLLM's `Waiting: N reqs`, it also reports
434	`no-queue vacancy`: sampled unfilled slots with no request waiting inside vLLM.
435	Use that as an admission-bubble proxy only; do not call it device idle time or
436	MFU.
437	
438	`n_tongyi.bash` now writes the same machine-readable result automatically to
439	`<PLANNING_LOG_DIR>/planning_core_time.json` after it stops the planning
440	servers. `PLANNING_LOG_DIR` defaults to the generated `logs/neuron-servers/`
441	directory, and `SUMMARIZE_NEURON_LOGS=0` disables this post-run step. The TP8
442	sidecar log is placed in that same directory so its heterogeneous capacity is
443	included in the summary.
444	
445	For the current TP16x3 planning topology, report both the 48-core planning
446	figure and the 64-core instance-effective figure. The latter cannot reach 100%
447	from Tongyi alone because embedding reserves cores `56-63` and cores `48-55`
448	are not assigned to this generation topology.
449	
450	The embedding service now exposes its process-lifetime batching counters at
451	`GET /metrics`:
452	
453	```bash
454	curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:6030/metrics | jq .
455	```
456	
457	`inference_busy_seconds / uptime_seconds` is the embedding batcher's wall-clock
458	busy duty; use it to decide whether the eight embedding cores are materially
459	busy before considering a different topology. It is not Tensor Engine
460	utilization or MFU, and it must not be added to planner MFU percentages.
461	
462	Use matched qids, clean output directories, and the same server warm-up policy
463	for a scheduler A/B. Keep every setting fixed except the lease scope:
464	
465	```bash
466	# Compile the isolated native/chunked TP16 artifact once on the Trn2 host.
467	NEURON_SCHEDULER=native \
468	  bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi_tp16.bash
469	
470	# Take the exact artifact emitted by that compile, then smoke it before a run.
471	# Replace <compile-timestamp> with the directory created by the command above.
472	NATIVE_ARTIFACT="$(cat logs/neuron-debug/tongyi-128k-prefix112-tp16-native-chunked-<compile-timestamp>/compiled_artifact_path)"
473	NEURON_SCHEDULER=native \
474	NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS="$NATIVE_ARTIFACT" \
475	TONGYI_REPLICAS=1 NUM_THREADS=1 MAX_QUERIES=1 RUN_EVAL=0 \
476	RUN_NAME=tongyi_native_smoke \
477	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
478	
479	# Legacy baseline
480	RUN_NAME=tongyi_lease_trajectory PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=trajectory \
481	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
482	
483	# Proposed scheduling mode: only with a separately compiled native artifact.
484	# The native compile prints a `compiled_artifact_path` file; copy its contents
485	# into NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS below.
486	NEURON_SCHEDULER=native \
487	NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS=/path/from/native-compile-log \
488	RUN_NAME=tongyi_lease_request PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=request \
489	  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
490	```
491	
492	`n_tongyi.bash` rejects unsafe custom + request/affinity combinations before it
493	starts replicas, rejects a missing artifact, and rejects the legacy custom
494	artifact in native mode. A native smoke passes only if the server logs show
495	`chunked_prefill=True` and contain neither `Prefill len ... exceeds largest
496	bucket` nor `EngineDeadError`.
497	
498	Compare q/min, vLLM slot duty, `total_endpoint_queue_wait`,
499	`planning_affinity_fallbacks`, `planning_endpoint_retirements`, and APC hit
500	rate. Do not compare prompt tokens/s alone: a high APC hit rate makes it look
501	artificially large. Each output JSON records the lease scope, affinity timeout,
502	client thread count, dispatch counts, queue wait, and endpoint retirements so
503	the two runs remain identifiable after completion. On an `EngineDeadError`, the
504	client removes that
505	endpoint from the in-process pool. Request leases retry a surviving replica;
506	custom trajectory leases instead fail the affected query to prevent a long
507	cold prompt from killing the survivor. If all replicas are dead, either mode
508	fails fast instead of spending the 150-minute trajectory budget on HTTP
509	retries. The client log's `q/min` is successful-query throughput;
510	`finished/min` is printed separately so a rapid failure cannot be misread as a
511	performance improvement.
512	
513	For a manually managed heterogeneous cluster, the same capacity-aware client
514	interface is available independently of the TP8 helper:
515	
516	```bash
517	--planning-ports 6008-6011 \
518	--planning-endpoint-capacities 6008:2,6009:2,6010:2,6011:1
519	```
520	
521	Real MFU requires a hardware trace. Do this only on an isolated stable replica
522	after an experiment, because inspection must be set before vLLM starts and can
523	perturb timings:
524	
525	```bash
526	PROFILE_DIR=/tmp/tongyi_mfu_$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)
527	export NEURON_RT_INSPECT_ENABLE=1
528	export NEURON_RT_INSPECT_DEVICE_PROFILE=1
529	export NEURON_RT_INSPECT_OUTPUT_DIR="$PROFILE_DIR"
530	# Start one stable TP16 replica, run a warmed representative request, then stop it.
531	
532	# Select the matching NEFF emitted under the inspection directory, then capture
533	# the second execution to skip warm-up. Keep this workload isolated: profiling
534	# perturbs scheduling and should not be combined with a throughput run.
535	NEFF=/path/to/neff_<id>_vnc_0.neff
536	neuron-explorer capture \
537	  -n "$NEFF" \
538	  -s "$PROFILE_DIR/profile.ntff" \
539	  --profile-nth-exec=2 \
540	  --enable-dge-notifs
541	neuron-explorer view --output-format summary-json \
542	  -n "$NEFF" \
543	  -s "$PROFILE_DIR/profile.ntff" > "$PROFILE_DIR/summary.json"
544	```
545	
546	Use the resulting Tensor Engine active-time percentage and trace duration as
547	the hardware utilization evidence. It is not interchangeable with slot duty;
548	compute model FLOPs / (available peak FLOPs x elapsed time) separately if an
549	end-to-end MFU value is required.
550	
551	## Evaluate with the Neuron judge
552	
553	For standalone evaluation, start the 16-replica Qwen judge cluster:
554	
555	```bash
556	bash bash/neuron/serve_qwen3_32b_judge_cluster.bash
557	```
558	
559	Then evaluate:
560	
561	```bash
562	INPUT_DIR=runs/qwen3-8b/tongyi_neuron \
563	  bash bash/neuron/evaluate_with_neuron_judge.bash
564	```
565	
566	Equivalent direct command:
567	
568	```bash
569	python scripts_evaluation/evaluate_run.py \
570	  --input_dir runs/qwen3-8b/tongyi_neuron \
571	  --model Qwen/Qwen3-32B \
572	  --judge-backend openai-compatible \
573	  --judge-base-urls http://127.0.0.1:8032/v1,http://127.0.0.1:8033/v1 \
574	  --judge-num-threads 2 \
575	  --batch_size 32
576	```
577	
578	The direct example uses two endpoints for brevity. Pass ports `8032-8047` and
579	`--judge-num-threads 16` to use the complete judge cluster. The legacy
580	`--judge-base-url` option remains supported for a single endpoint.
581	
582	## Current compatibility notes
583	
584	- `scripts_evaluation/evaluate_run.py` supports both `local-vllm` and
585	  `openai-compatible` judge backends.
586	- Tongyi uses Chat Completions through localhost and is the first Trn2 agent path.
587	- Tongyi defaults to `MAX_MODEL_LEN=128000`, `MAX_NUM_SEQS=2`,
588	  `BLOCK_SIZE=32`, prefix caching enabled, and chunked prefill disabled.
589	  This requires 8000 effective KV blocks:
590	  `ceil(128000 / 32) * 2`. vLLM-Neuron internally adds one reserved null
591	  block to the CLI override.
592	- The default asymmetric APC profile uses context/suffix buckets
593	  `[16384]`, prefix bucket `[114688]`, and token-generation buckets
594	  `[16384, 32768, 65536, 98304, 128000]`. NxD also adds the zero-prefix
595	  case. The largest compiled context pair is exactly
596	  `16384 + 114688 = 131072`, the current NKI attention limit. Using one fixed
597	  suffix bucket avoids the current NxD selector failure where a small suffix
598	  bucket is selected before discovering that a long cached prefix does not fit.
599	- Tongyi's `get_document` tool accepts a zero-based `chunk_index` and returns
600	  at most 30000 document-text tokens per call. Responses include
601	  `total_chunks`, `chunk_tokens`, and `has_more`; increment
602	  `chunk_index` while `has_more` is true. The limit is configurable through
603	  `--document-chunk-tokens`.
604	- This profile compiles two context-encoding combinations after NxD adds the
605	  zero-prefix case. It supports any prompt up to 128000 tokens provided the
606	  uncached suffix fits the fixed 16384-token bucket.
607	  `n_tongyi_get.bash` defaults `DOCUMENT_CHUNK_TOKENS` to 12000 to leave room
608	  for tool-call framing and other uncached tokens.
609	- `bash/neuron/compile_tongyi_tp16.bash` provides an isolated TP=16 profile
610	  with a `[16384]` suffix and a single `[114688]` prefix bucket. NxD also
611	  adds the zero-prefix case, so this produces two context-encoding HLOs. It
612	  uses NeuronCores `8-23`, port `6016`, separate compiler
613	  work/cache directories, and 16 HLO compile workers by default, so it can run
614	  alongside the default TP=8 compiler. With
615	  `NEURON_SCHEDULER=native`, it automatically switches to separate
616	  `*-native-chunked` work/cache/lock paths and tag
617	  `tongyi-tp16-native-chunked-v1`; never reuse the custom artifact for that
618	  experiment. Start its artifact with `bash/neuron/serve_tongyi_tp16.bash`.
619	- The launcher validates bucket coverage before starting vLLM. Profiles whose
620	  largest suffix/prefix pair exceeds 131072, or cannot cover
621	  `MAX_MODEL_LEN`, fail immediately instead of failing late in NKI tracing.
622	- `search_agent/oss_client.py` uses the OpenAI Responses API. Only use it with a
623	  Neuron vLLM build that supports Responses API, or add a chat-completions OSS
624	  adapter before running GPT-OSS agent experiments.
625	- FAISS retrieval defaults to CPU on Trn2 through `faiss-cpu`; do not expect
626	  FAISS GPU or CUDA embedding acceleration on Trainium.
627

file.content:
# AWS Trn2 Neuron DLAMI runbook

This repo treats AWS Trn2 as a local OpenAI-compatible inference service. The
Python project environment stays CPU/client-side; Neuron vLLM is launched from
the Neuron DLAMI environment.

**NEW**: We now support **Docker-based deployment** using the official AWS Neuron Deep Learning Container. See [Neuron Docker Quick Start](neuron_docker_quickstart.md) for the simplified Docker workflow, or continue reading for the native DLAMI setup.

Official references:

- AWS Neuron vLLM user guide: https://awsdocs-neuron.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/libraries/nxd-inference/developer_guides/vllm-user-guide.html
- AWS Neuron PyTorch setup: https://awsdocs-neuron.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/general/setup/torch-neuronx.html
- AWS Trn2 architecture: https://awsdocs-neuron.readthedocs-hosted.com/en/latest/about-neuron/arch/neuron-hardware/trn2-arch.html
- AWS Neuron DLC: https://github.com/aws-neuron/deep-learning-containers

## Environment

Use the default repo environment for clients, retrieval, and evaluation control:

```bash
uv sync
source .venv/bin/activate
```

Do not install `flash-attn`, CUDA vLLM, or CUDA Docker images in this default
environment. The old CUDA setup remains available with:

```bash
uv sync --extra cuda
```

On the DLAMI, verify Neuron visibility before serving models:

```bash
neuron-ls
neuron-top
```

## Serve required models

Each script preserves the existing model id. If Neuron vLLM cannot compile or
serve that model, treat it as an unsupported-model blocker; do not silently swap
models or route to an external API.

```bash
bash bash/neuron/serve_tongyi.bash
bash bash/neuron/serve_gpt_oss_20b.bash
bash bash/neuron/serve_gpt_oss_120b.bash
bash bash/neuron/serve_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
```

Common overrides:

```bash
TP_SIZE=64 MAX_MODEL_LEN=32768 MAX_NUM_SEQS=1 PORT=8032 \
  bash bash/neuron/serve_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
```

The generic launcher is `bash/neuron/serve_vllm_neuron.bash`. It sets
`VLLM_NEURON_FRAMEWORK=neuronx-distributed-inference` and calls
`python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server`. The Neuron vLLM platform
plugin selects the Neuron backend.

The wrapper scripts and generic launcher accept these environment variables:

- `MODEL`: Hugging Face model id or an explicit local checkpoint path.
- `SERVED_MODEL_NAME`: model name exposed by the OpenAI-compatible API. It
  defaults to the original `MODEL`, even when weights are loaded from the
  repo-local cache.
- `TOKENIZER`: tokenizer id or path. Auto-cached model weights default to the
  original model id because NxD's model serialization does not save tokenizer
  files.
- `HOST` and `PORT`: listen address and TCP port.
- `TP_SIZE`: number of NeuronCores used for tensor parallelism. It is part of
  the compiled-artifact cache key.
- `MAX_MODEL_LEN`: compiled context capacity; larger values make compilation
  and KV-cache requirements grow.
- `MAX_NUM_SEQS`: maximum concurrent sequences. This is compile-time capacity,
  not the client's thread count.
- `BLOCK_SIZE`: vLLM KV-cache block size.
- `NUM_GPU_BLOCKS_OVERRIDE`: effective vLLM KV-block capacity before the
  Neuron plugin adds its reserved null block.
- `ENABLE_PREFIX_CACHING`: pass the explicit vLLM prefix-caching switch.
- `ENABLE_CHUNKED_PREFILL`: pass the explicit vLLM chunked-prefill switch.
- `NEURON_SCHEDULER`: Tongyi-only scheduler profile. `custom` (the default)
  uses Neuron continuous batching and disables chunked prefill. `native` sets
  `DISABLE_NEURON_CUSTOM_SCHEDULER=1`, enables chunked prefill, and defaults
  `MAX_NUM_BATCHED_TOKENS` to `16384` to match the compiled uncached-context
  bucket. Compile and validate `native` with a separate artifact/cache before
  using it in a run; an explicit `--max-num-batched-tokens` in
  `EXTRA_VLLM_ARGS` takes precedence.
- `OVERRIDE_NEURON_CONFIG`: JSON object passed through vLLM's
  `additional_config.override_neuron_config` interface to NxD.
- `EXTRA_VLLM_ARGS`: additional vLLM CLI arguments. Keep quoting simple because
  the launcher splits this string into shell words.
- `NEURON_CACHE_TAG`: manual compiled-cache invalidation tag.
- `MAX_NUM_BATCHED_TOKENS`: native-scheduler chunked-prefill limit. Leave it
  at the default `16384` for the TP16 128k profile unless testing a separately
  compiled bucket configuration.
- `NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS`: advanced explicit compiled-artifact directory
  override.
- `NXD_COMPILE_MAX_WORKERS`: maximum concurrent NxD HLO compiler jobs. The
  Tongyi precompile wrapper defaults to `1`; direct serving leaves NxD unchanged
  unless this variable is set. The launcher installs the limit inside vLLM's
  spawned EngineCore process, where NxD creates the compiler executor. The
  default compile profile serializes HLO compilation to reduce memory pressure.
- `NEURON_COMPILE_MEMORY_HIGH` and `NEURON_COMPILE_MEMORY_MAX`: soft and hard
  cgroup limits used by `compile_tongyi.bash`. They default to `1750G` and
  `1900G` on the 2 TiB Trn2.48xlarge host.
- `NEURON_COMPILE_USE_SYSTEMD_SCOPE`: set to `0` only when the compile already
  runs under an equivalent cgroup limit. It defaults to `1`.
- `PYTHON_BIN`: Python executable from the Neuron environment.

The server exposes the OpenAI-compatible HTTP interface. Readiness/model
identity is available at `GET /v1/models`; Tongyi and the judge use
`POST /v1/chat/completions`. `n_tongyi.bash` starts each server, waits for its
`/v1/models` endpoint, then runs the client or evaluator against that base URL.

## Model and compilation caches

The generic launcher keeps both cache layers under the ignored
`local-models/` directory:

- Hugging Face weights are stored at `local-models/<model-id>/`. Once the config
  and either a single weight file or a weight index with every referenced shard
  exist, later launches use that checkpoint instead of loading and saving the
  remote model again.
  Tongyi's tokenizer remains on the original model id because its locally
  serialized model directory contains weights but not tokenizer files. The
  Qwen3-32B download includes tokenizer files, and judge replicas use that local
  tokenizer path.
- Traced and compiled Neuron models are stored under
  `local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/<configuration-hash>/`. The hash
  includes the model id, tensor parallel size, sequence length, concurrency,
  block size, Neuron overrides, extra vLLM arguments, cache tag, and relevant
  package versions.

The first launch still performs weight conversion and Neuron compilation.
Later launches with exactly the same model and compilation settings reuse the
compiled artifacts. Changing a compilation setting creates a separate cache
directory instead of reusing an incompatible model.

Precompile Tongyi:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi.bash
```

The wrapper serializes Tongyi precompiles with `flock` and runs the complete
compiler process tree in a user systemd scope. The hard memory limit terminates
the compile rather than allowing concurrent `neuronx-cc` processes to make the
instance unresponsive. It writes timestamped `compile.log`, `kernel.log`, and
PID files under `logs/neuron-debug/`. Compile work and the persistent compiler
cache live under `local-models/` on `/dev3`, avoiding the root filesystem.
The priority HLO and graph tracing remain serial; the default profile keeps HLO
compilation serial to reduce peak memory.

Before compiling, the wrapper moves inactive failed compiler-cache modules to a
timestamped sibling `.failed-*` directory. It does not touch complete modules
or a module whose lock is held by an active compiler. An incomplete repo-local
compiled-artifact directory is similarly renamed with a `.failed-<timestamp>`
suffix before the clean retry.

The startup log reports a compiled cache as `cold` when the directory is empty,
`incomplete` when an interrupted compile left partial files, and `ready` only
when NxD's complete `model.pt` artifact exists. NxD still performs its own load
validation before serving requests.

Set `NEURON_CACHE_TAG` when a model revision or local compiler-related change
is not represented by the normal launch arguments:

```bash
NEURON_CACHE_TAG=tongyi-revision-2 bash bash/neuron/serve_tongyi.bash
```

`NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS` remains an advanced explicit override. Use it only
for one model and one fixed configuration; vLLM Neuron loads a valid artifact
from that path even when the current launch arguments differ. Do not point
Tongyi and the Qwen judge at the same explicit directory.

## Smoke test a server

```bash
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:8032/v1/models

python - <<'PY'
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url="http://127.0.0.1:8032/v1", api_key="EMPTY")
print(client.chat.completions.create(
    model="Qwen/Qwen3-32B",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Return exactly: ok"}],
    max_tokens=8,
).choices[0].message.content)
PY
```

## Run Tongyi agent on Trn2

Compile the three models sequentially. Do not run two compile scripts at the
same time: each compiler can use most of the host's memory.

Compile Tongyi once:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi.bash
```

Use compile-only mode when NeuronCores are already serving an experiment:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi_tp16.bash --compile-only
```

This still starts vLLM's EngineCore as the NxDI configuration and compilation
entry point, but exits after `model.pt` and any configured pre-sharded weights
are written. It does not load the artifact onto NeuronCores, warm up the model,
or start the temporary HTTP server. The default mode retains end-to-end load
validation. Tongyi enables `save_sharded_checkpoint` by default, so a successful
compile also writes `weights/tp*_sharded_checkpoint.safetensors`; subsequent
servers load those TP shards instead of re-reading and sharding the full
Hugging Face checkpoint.

The compiler writes the validated artifact directory to
`logs/neuron-debug/<compile-tag>-<timestamp>/compiled_artifact_path` and prints
it at completion. Use that exact path for a native runtime; do not infer it
from a cache directory or reuse the custom artifact.

The default Tongyi artifact uses TP=8, `MAX_MODEL_LEN=128000`, and
`MAX_NUM_SEQS=2`, with context/suffix bucket `[16384]`, prefix
`[114688]`, and generation
`[16384, 32768, 65536, 98304, 128000]`.

After Tongyi finishes, set up and compile the dedicated Qwen3 embedding
environment:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/setup_qwen3_embedding_env.bash
bash bash/neuron/download_qwen3_embedding.bash
bash bash/neuron/compile_qwen3_embedding.bash
```

After the embedding compile finishes, download and compile the judge:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/download_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
bash bash/neuron/compile_qwen3_32b_judge.bash
```

Do not start the experiment until all three compile scripts have completed.
Run a one-query smoke profile first:

```bash
MAX_QUERIES=1 \
TONGYI_REPLICAS=1 \
JUDGE_REPLICAS=1 \
NUM_THREADS=1 \
JUDGE_NUM_THREADS=1 \
RUN_NAME=neuron_smoke \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
```

Then run the formal throughput profile:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
```

This profile enables `--filter-all` and registers only the `search` tool; it
does not enable `get_document`. The default run name is
`tongyi_filter_all_no_get`.

The current full profile defaults to 18 client threads and starts three TP16
Tongyi replicas on ports `6008-6010`, using NeuronCores `0-47`; the TP8
Qwen3-Embedding-8B service uses cores `56-63`. Replica processes are
NUMA-bound and at most two replicas load concurrently. Each endpoint has two
LLM-call slots. With the current `custom` scheduler, the full profile defaults
to `PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=trajectory`: its 16k uncached-context bucket cannot
safely serve a long cold history after an APC miss. This is not merely an
endpoint-routing constraint. Completed requests release their KV blocks into
the endpoint's evictable prefix cache; with 8000 32-token blocks, a TP16
replica has room for only two 128k histories. Releasing six long custom
trajectories per endpoint would let their APC entries evict each other. The
separately compiled `native`/chunked profile defaults to
`PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=request`, which reserves a slot only for an LLM HTTP call
and releases it while the agent retrieves documents. That mode prefers the
trajectory's home endpoint for 0.2 seconds by default
(`PLANNING_AFFINITY_TIMEOUT`) before routing to another available replica.

The lease scopes have intentionally different failure behavior. In
`custom`/`trajectory` mode, an `EngineDeadError` (or a failed post-connection
`/v1/models` probe) retires the dead endpoint and marks that query as
`[Failed]`; it never retries that long trajectory on a different replica,
which would make another cold prefill and can cascade the same crash. In
`native`/`request` mode, the failed call is retried on a surviving endpoint.
New trajectories skip retired endpoints in both modes, and all-retired pools
fail fast rather than consuming the 150-minute per-query timeout.
Failed output JSONs retain the error and `status: "failed"`, so a later clean
invocation reruns them instead of treating them as completed cached qids. The
resume scan also retries legacy empty `No answer found after 2h30mins` outputs,
which is how the old client represented a dead endpoint after exhausting HTTP
retries.

The 2026-07-15 custom-scheduler run provides a useful but incomplete baseline:
before the replicas failed, its 10-second vLLM samples had 84.68% aggregate
slot duty (4510 occupied slots / 5326 possible slots). Per-replica duty was
79.35% (6008), 83.18% (6009), and 87.14% (6010). This shows that 18 client
threads were already enough to keep most of the six admitted LLM slots busy;
more threads alone cannot remove the remaining bubbles and cannot repair a
dead endpoint. It is historical slot-duty evidence only, not MFU or a valid
end-to-end throughput result: all three replicas later exited after a long
cold prefill exceeded the 16k suffix bucket.

The detailed vLLM samples make the remaining limitation concrete: of the
53,202 sampled slot-seconds, **8,130 slot-seconds (15.28%)** had an unfilled
slot while `Waiting: 0 reqs`. This is a client/admission-bubble proxy: vLLM had
no request queued to use the empty slot, consistent with trajectories retaining
their endpoint capacity while fetching documents. It is not Tensor Engine idle
time. The remaining 29,718 slot-seconds were unobserved/dead capacity after
replica failure. A custom affinity lease is unsafe for this profile: returning
to the same endpoint does not pin its completed KV blocks, so more than two
long histories can evict each other and recreate the cold-prefill failure.
`n_tongyi.bash` rejects it before server start. The substantive safe step for
removing the live bubble is a verified native/request scheduler A/B.

When the same logs are measured over one common window—from first planning
traffic at 03:40:30 to the last surviving replica's crash at 07:30:50—the
failure-inclusive planning slot-equivalent duty is only **54.34%**. This is the
right figure for lost planning core-time; the 84.68% figure only answers how
busy replicas were while each one was still alive. The corresponding **40.76%**
instance figure excludes the embedding service because it has no vLLM
`Running` log. Neither is MFU.

### Fill the final eight planning cores (custom trajectory mode)

The 3xTP16 planning topology leaves NeuronCores `48-55` unassigned. A verified
TP8, `MAX_NUM_SEQS=1`, 128k artifact exists at
`local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/9a36595723d1059555571d3bf524fa88a507814a2efbe2917865b6350bb75912`.
Set `ENABLE_TP8_SIDECAR=1` to start it on port `6011` and cores `48-55` after
the three TP16 replicas and embedding service are ready. Its one slot is
advertised separately, so the client schedules `6008:2,6009:2,6010:2,6011:1`
rather than over-admitting the sidecar. This raises planning capacity from six
to seven admitted sequences and assigns all 64 cores (56 planning + 8
embedding).

```bash
ENABLE_TP8_SIDECAR=1 RUN_NAME=tongyi_tp16x3_tp8x1 \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
```

This is deliberately restricted to `NEURON_SCHEDULER=custom` with
`PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=trajectory`: a request-level lease could send a long
history to the TP8 replica without its APC state and reproduce the cold-prefill
crash. Do not enable it for the native/request A/B until a separate native TP8
artifact has passed smoke testing.

The original TP4, 16k-suffix profile is not viable: its 2026-07-15 compile
failed with `NCC_EOOM002`, reporting 31.17 GB peak HBM against the 24.00 GB
Trn2 partition limit. A narrower TP4×1 profile with an 8k suffix and 120k
prefix did later compile to
`local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/27b98ea6f3cabed3fd9ed630d1a2996e28998618d11e43538edbd3d3e09c3586`.
An even narrower 4k suffix / 124k prefix artifact also compiled to
`local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/1564e9cb322946a1b12565ab27e5c4b63f2c8d1ee73a0f8b0111c01a6fdc1fd2`,
but the 8k profile is the better first experiment because a search tool result
can exceed a 4k uncached suffix. Both compiles used `--compile-only`, so neither
is a validated runtime topology. Smoke the 8k artifact on one copy before
considering two TP4 sidecars on cores `48-51` and `52-55`:

```bash
PORT=6011 NEURON_RT_VISIBLE_CORES=48-51 \
NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS=local-models/neuron-compiled-artifacts/27b98ea6f3cabed3fd9ed630d1a2996e28998618d11e43538edbd3d3e09c3586 \
NEURON_REQUIRE_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS=1 \
  bash bash/neuron/serve_tongyi_tp4_s1.bash
```

Only after a warm request succeeds without an HBM allocation failure or a
`Prefill len ... exceeds largest bucket` error should TP4×2 be compared with
the proven TP8×1 sidecar. `ENABLE_TP4_SIDECARS=1` starts one TP4 sidecar on each
four-core range, advertises their one-slot capacities separately, and is
mutually exclusive with the TP8 helper:

```bash
ENABLE_TP4_SIDECARS=1 NUM_THREADS=18 RUN_EVAL=0 \
RUN_NAME=tongyi_tp16x3_tp4x2 \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
```

This turns the same eight otherwise unused planning cores into two slots, for
eight total planning slots (versus seven for TP8×1 and six without a sidecar).
It is an experimental throughput A/B, not a claimed speedup: TP4 may have
lower per-slot speed, so compare successful q/min, no-queue vacancy, and
slot-equivalent core-time before adopting it.

For an externally managed A/B, start all planning servers first, then use
`START_SERVER=0`; the runner waits for every port listed in `PLANNING_PORTS`
before launching clients:

```bash
START_SERVER=0 PLANNING_PORTS=6008-6012 \
PLANNING_ENDPOINT_CAPACITIES=6008:2,6009:2,6010:2,6011:1,6012:1 \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
```

FAISS remains CPU-side, while query embeddings come from the local Neuron HTTP
service. Override `START_SERVER=0` only when all planning and embedding
endpoints are already running. After generation completes, `n_tongyi.bash`
stops the Tongyi and embedding cluster, starts 16 independent TP4 judge
replicas on ports `8032-8047` using all 64 NeuronCores, and evaluates with 16
concurrent judge requests. Generation and judge replicas never occupy the
NeuronCores at the same time.

### Measure planning core-time and MFU

`Running: N reqs` in a vLLM server log measures admitted request slots, not
Neuron-core utilization. Report it as **slot duty** only:

```text
slot duty = sum(Running) / (MAX_NUM_SEQS * number of samples)
```

Use the checked-in summarizer for an experiment-window calculation that counts
time after a crashed replica as zero duty:

```bash
python scripts/neuron_summarize_vllm_logs.py --year 2026 \
  logs/neuron-servers/<run>/tongyi-6008.log \
  logs/neuron-servers/<run>/tongyi-6009.log \
  logs/neuron-servers/<run>/tongyi-6010.log
```

It reports **slot-equivalent core-time**, not MFU. It infers at most 30 seconds
from a scheduler sample and uses the first and final samples as the default
window; pass explicit `--window-start` and `--window-end` if the experiment
window is wider. When a log includes vLLM's `Waiting: N reqs`, it also reports
`no-queue vacancy`: sampled unfilled slots with no request waiting inside vLLM.
Use that as an admission-bubble proxy only; do not call it device idle time or
MFU.

`n_tongyi.bash` now writes the same machine-readable result automatically to
`<PLANNING_LOG_DIR>/planning_core_time.json` after it stops the planning
servers. `PLANNING_LOG_DIR` defaults to the generated `logs/neuron-servers/`
directory, and `SUMMARIZE_NEURON_LOGS=0` disables this post-run step. The TP8
sidecar log is placed in that same directory so its heterogeneous capacity is
included in the summary.

For the current TP16x3 planning topology, report both the 48-core planning
figure and the 64-core instance-effective figure. The latter cannot reach 100%
from Tongyi alone because embedding reserves cores `56-63` and cores `48-55`
are not assigned to this generation topology.

The embedding service now exposes its process-lifetime batching counters at
`GET /metrics`:

```bash
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:6030/metrics | jq .
```

`inference_busy_seconds / uptime_seconds` is the embedding batcher's wall-clock
busy duty; use it to decide whether the eight embedding cores are materially
busy before considering a different topology. It is not Tensor Engine
utilization or MFU, and it must not be added to planner MFU percentages.

Use matched qids, clean output directories, and the same server warm-up policy
for a scheduler A/B. Keep every setting fixed except the lease scope:

```bash
# Compile the isolated native/chunked TP16 artifact once on the Trn2 host.
NEURON_SCHEDULER=native \
  bash bash/neuron/compile_tongyi_tp16.bash

# Take the exact artifact emitted by that compile, then smoke it before a run.
# Replace <compile-timestamp> with the directory created by the command above.
NATIVE_ARTIFACT="$(cat logs/neuron-debug/tongyi-128k-prefix112-tp16-native-chunked-<compile-timestamp>/compiled_artifact_path)"
NEURON_SCHEDULER=native \
NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS="$NATIVE_ARTIFACT" \
TONGYI_REPLICAS=1 NUM_THREADS=1 MAX_QUERIES=1 RUN_EVAL=0 \
RUN_NAME=tongyi_native_smoke \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash

# Legacy baseline
RUN_NAME=tongyi_lease_trajectory PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=trajectory \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash

# Proposed scheduling mode: only with a separately compiled native artifact.
# The native compile prints a `compiled_artifact_path` file; copy its contents
# into NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS below.
NEURON_SCHEDULER=native \
NEURON_COMPILED_ARTIFACTS=/path/from/native-compile-log \
RUN_NAME=tongyi_lease_request PLANNING_LEASE_SCOPE=request \
  bash bash/neuron/n_tongyi.bash
```

`n_tongyi.bash` rejects unsafe custom + request/affinity combinations before it
starts replicas, rejects a missing artifact, and rejects the legacy custom
artifact in native mode. A native smoke passes only if the server logs show
`chunked_prefill=True` and contain neither `Prefill len ... exceeds largest
bucket` nor `EngineDeadError`.

Compare q/min, vLLM slot duty, `total_endpoint_queue_wait`,
`planning_affinity_fallbacks`, `planning_endpoint_retirements`, and APC hit
rate. Do not compare prompt tokens/s alone: a high APC hit rate makes it look
artificially large. Each output JSON records the lease scope, affinity timeout,
client thread count, dispatch counts, queue wait, and endpoint retirements so
the two runs remain identifiable after completion. On an `EngineDeadError`, the
client removes that
endpoint from the in-process pool. Request leases retry a surviving replica;
custom trajectory leases instead fail the affected query to prevent a long
cold prompt from killing the survivor. If all replicas are dead, either mode
fails fast instead of spending the 150-minute trajectory budget on HTTP
retries. The client log's `q/min` is successful-query throughput;
`finished/min` is printed separately so a rapid failure cannot be misread as a
performance improvement.

For a manually managed heterogeneous cluster, the same capacity-aware client
interface is available independently of the TP8 helper:

```bash
--planning-ports 6008-6011 \
--planning-endpoint-capacities 6008:2,6009:2,6010:2,6011:1
```

Real MFU requires a hardware trace. Do this only on an isolated stable replica
after an experiment, because inspection must be set before vLLM starts and can
perturb timings:

```bash
PROFILE_DIR=/tmp/tongyi_mfu_$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)
export NEURON_RT_INSPECT_ENABLE=1
export NEURON_RT_INSPECT_DEVICE_PROFILE=1
export NEURON_RT_INSPECT_OUTPUT_DIR="$PROFILE_DIR"
# Start one stable TP16 replica, run a warmed representative request, then stop it.

# Select the matching NEFF emitted under the inspection directory, then capture
# the second execution to skip warm-up. Keep this workload isolated: profiling
# perturbs scheduling and should not be combined with a throughput run.
NEFF=/path/to/neff_<id>_vnc_0.neff
neuron-explorer capture \
  -n "$NEFF" \
  -s "$PROFILE_DIR/profile.ntff" \
  --profile-nth-exec=2 \
  --enable-dge-notifs
neuron-explorer view --output-format summary-json \
  -n "$NEFF" \
  -s "$PROFILE_DIR/profile.ntff" > "$PROFILE_DIR/summary.json"
```

Use the resulting Tensor Engine active-time percentage and trace duration as
the hardware utilization evidence. It is not interchangeable with slot duty;
compute model FLOPs / (available peak FLOPs x elapsed time) separately if an
end-to-end MFU value is required.

## Evaluate with the Neuron judge

For standalone evaluation, start the 16-replica Qwen judge cluster:

```bash
bash bash/neuron/serve_qwen3_32b_judge_cluster.bash
```

Then evaluate:

```bash
INPUT_DIR=runs/qwen3-8b/tongyi_neuron \
  bash bash/neuron/evaluate_with_neuron_judge.bash
```

Equivalent direct command:

```bash
python scripts_evaluation/evaluate_run.py \
  --input_dir runs/qwen3-8b/tongyi_neuron \
  --model Qwen/Qwen3-32B \
  --judge-backend openai-compatible \
  --judge-base-urls http://127.0.0.1:8032/v1,http://127.0.0.1:8033/v1 \
  --judge-num-threads 2 \
  --batch_size 32
```

The direct example uses two endpoints for brevity. Pass ports `8032-8047` and
`--judge-num-threads 16` to use the complete judge cluster. The legacy
`--judge-base-url` option remains supported for a single endpoint.

## Current compatibility notes

- `scripts_evaluation/evaluate_run.py` supports both `local-vllm` and
  `openai-compatible` judge backends.
- Tongyi uses Chat Completions through localhost and is the first Trn2 agent path.
- Tongyi defaults to `MAX_MODEL_LEN=128000`, `MAX_NUM_SEQS=2`,
  `BLOCK_SIZE=32`, prefix caching enabled, and chunked prefill disabled.
  This requires 8000 effective KV blocks:
  `ceil(128000 / 32) * 2`. vLLM-Neuron internally adds one reserved null
  block to the CLI override.
- The default asymmetric APC profile uses context/suffix buckets
  `[16384]`, prefix bucket `[114688]`, and token-generation buckets
  `[16384, 32768, 65536, 98304, 128000]`. NxD also adds the zero-prefix
  case. The largest compiled context pair is exactly
  `16384 + 114688 = 131072`, the current NKI attention limit. Using one fixed
  suffix bucket avoids the current NxD selector failure where a small suffix
  bucket is selected before discovering that a long cached prefix does not fit.
- Tongyi's `get_document` tool accepts a zero-based `chunk_index` and returns
  at most 30000 document-text tokens per call. Responses include
  `total_chunks`, `chunk_tokens`, and `has_more`; increment
  `chunk_index` while `has_more` is true. The limit is configurable through
  `--document-chunk-tokens`.
- This profile compiles two context-encoding combinations after NxD adds the
  zero-prefix case. It supports any prompt up to 128000 tokens provided the
  uncached suffix fits the fixed 16384-token bucket.
  `n_tongyi_get.bash` defaults `DOCUMENT_CHUNK_TOKENS` to 12000 to leave room
  for tool-call framing and other uncached tokens.
- `bash/neuron/compile_tongyi_tp16.bash` provides an isolated TP=16 profile
  with a `[16384]` suffix and a single `[114688]` prefix bucket. NxD also
  adds the zero-prefix case, so this produces two context-encoding HLOs. It
  uses NeuronCores `8-23`, port `6016`, separate compiler
  work/cache directories, and 16 HLO compile workers by default, so it can run
  alongside the default TP=8 compiler. With
  `NEURON_SCHEDULER=native`, it automatically switches to separate
  `*-native-chunked` work/cache/lock paths and tag
  `tongyi-tp16-native-chunked-v1`; never reuse the custom artifact for that
  experiment. Start its artifact with `bash/neuron/serve_tongyi_tp16.bash`.
- The launcher validates bucket coverage before starting vLLM. Profiles whose
  largest suffix/prefix pair exceeds 131072, or cannot cover
  `MAX_MODEL_LEN`, fail immediately instead of failing late in NKI tracing.
- `search_agent/oss_client.py` uses the OpenAI Responses API. Only use it with a
  Neuron vLLM build that supports Responses API, or add a chat-completions OSS
  adapter before running GPT-OSS agent experiments.
- FAISS retrieval defaults to CPU on Trn2 through `faiss-cpu`; do not expect
  FAISS GPU or CUDA embedding acceleration on Trainium.

/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:- `TOKENIZER`: tokenizer id or path. Auto-cached model weights default to the
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  original model id because NxD's model serialization does not save tokenizer
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:- `NUM_GPU_BLOCKS_OVERRIDE`: effective vLLM KV-block capacity before the
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  using it in a run; an explicit `--max-num-batched-tokens` in
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  Tongyi's tokenizer remains on the original model id because its locally
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  serialized model directory contains weights but not tokenizer files. The
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  Qwen3-32B download includes tokenizer files, and judge replicas use that local
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  tokenizer path.
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:    max_tokens=8,
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:Then run the formal throughput profile:
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:the endpoint's evictable prefix cache; with 8000 32-token blocks, a TP16
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:end-to-end throughput result: all three replicas later exited after a long
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:It is an experimental throughput A/B, not a claimed speedup: TP4 may have
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:rate. Do not compare prompt tokens/s alone: a high APC hit rate makes it look
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:retries. The client log's `q/min` is successful-query throughput;
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:# perturbs scheduling and should not be combined with a throughput run.
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  `[16384]`, prefix bucket `[114688]`, and token-generation buckets
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  at most 30000 document-text tokens per call. Responses include
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  `total_chunks`, `chunk_tokens`, and `has_more`; increment
/dev3/zigeng/BrowseComp-Plus/docs/neuron_trn2.md:  `--document-chunk-tokens`.
